walkthrough.title: Give Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Harmonies! Give each player one personal hex board. This is your landscape canvas where you will stack colorful tokens to build a three-dimensional world. Place it in front of you with plenty of space around it.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Token Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the bag of landscape tokens in the center of the table. These wooden discs come in five colors. Green for trees and forests, yellow for fields, gray for mountains, brown for buildings, and blue for water. Make sure everyone can reach the bag.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Central Pool
walkthrough.text: Draw tokens from the bag to fill the central display. Tokens are arranged in groups of three. The number of groups depends on the player count. This display is where everyone will draft tokens on their turns.

walkthrough.title: Deal Animal Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the animal cards and deal a selection face up. Each player selects animal cards they will try to complete during the game. These cards show specific three-dimensional patterns of colored tokens that score victory points when you match them on your board.

walkthrough.title: Optional Landscape Bonus Cards
walkthrough.text: If you want extra scoring variety, add landscape bonus cards. These provide additional points for terrain arrangements beyond the animal patterns. For your first game, you can skip these and just play with animal cards.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a first player randomly or choose the youngest player. Play proceeds clockwise. Everyone starts with an empty board and no tokens. You are ready to build your landscape!

summary.title: Give Out Player Boards
bullets: 1 hex board per player
Empty board — no starting tokens

Place token bag centrally

summary.title: Fill the Central Pool

Number of groups scales with player count

summary.title: Deal Animal Cards
bullets: Shuffle and deal animal cards
Each player selects scoring targets
Cards show 3D token patterns to build

summary.title: Optional Landscape Bonus Cards

summary.title: Choose First Player
bullets: Random or youngest player
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do two things. First, take exactly three tokens from one group in the central display. Second, place all three tokens on your personal board, one at a time. Then the display is refilled for the next player.

walkthrough.title: Drafting Tokens
walkthrough.text: You must take all three tokens from a single group. You cannot mix and match from different groups. Look at the available groups and choose the one whose colors best match the animal patterns you are trying to build. Anticipate what your opponents need and consider taking groups they want.

walkthrough.title: Placing Tokens on Your Board
walkthrough.text: Place each token one at a time on your hex board. Tokens can go on empty spaces at ground level, or they can stack on top of existing tokens. The maximum height is three tokens tall. When you stack, the top color is what counts for pattern matching from above.

walkthrough.title: Stacking Rules
walkthrough.text: Level one tokens sit directly on the board. Level two tokens must rest on a level one token beneath them. Level three tokens go on top of a level two stack. You cannot skip levels. This vertical dimension is what makes Harmonies unique.

walkthrough.title: Animal Card Patterns
walkthrough.text: Animal cards show patterns of colored tokens as seen from above your board. Some animals also require specific heights. A mountain eagle might need gray tokens at level three. A pond fish might need blue at level one. When your board matches a pattern completely with the right colors and heights, claim that animal card for its victory points.

walkthrough.title: Completing Animal Patterns
walkthrough.text: When all tokens in an animal card's pattern are in the correct positions, colors, and heights on your board, you score that card immediately. The tokens stay on your board and can potentially contribute to other patterns too. More complex patterns are worth more victory points.

walkthrough.title: Covering and Changing the Landscape
walkthrough.text: Here is a key subtlety. When you stack a token on top of another, you change the visible color of that space. This can complete one pattern but break another. Think carefully before stacking because once a token is placed, it cannot be moved.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the token supply is exhausted and the central pool cannot be fully refilled. All players complete an equal number of turns. Add up victory points from completed animal cards and any landscape bonus cards. The player with the most points wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Harmonies
walkthrough.text: We will walk through several turns with three players, showing how to draft, place, stack, and score animal patterns. Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3 each have empty boards and a selection of animal cards to complete. Let's build some landscapes!

walkthrough.title: Choosing Your Animal Cards
walkthrough.text: Before we start, let's look at our animal cards. Player 1 picks a deer that needs three adjacent green tokens at level one and a butterfly that needs two yellow tokens at level one. Start with simpler patterns for your first game. Complex multi-height animals can wait until you are comfortable with the basics.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's First Draft
walkthrough.text: Player 1 looks at the central display and finds a group with two green tokens and one yellow token. Perfect for the deer pattern! Take all three tokens from that group. Now place them on the board: two green tokens adjacent to each other, and the yellow nearby for the butterfly pattern.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Drafts and Places
walkthrough.text: Player 2 is working on a mountain animal that needs gray tokens at level three. They take a group with two gray tokens and one brown token. Player 2 places one gray on the board and the brown token next to it. The second gray goes on top of a gray already placed from a previous thought. Wait, they have not placed anything yet! They place all three at ground level for now.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Random Placement
walkthrough.text: Player 3 takes a group of tokens and places them scattered across the board with no particular plan. None of the tokens are adjacent to each other, and they do not connect to any animal pattern. Three tokens have been placed but zero progress has been made toward scoring.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Place with Purpose
walkthrough.text: Every token you place should advance at least one animal pattern. Random placement wastes your limited tokens. Before placing, look at your animal cards and identify which spaces need which colors. Place tokens that serve multiple patterns simultaneously when possible.

walkthrough.title: Building Height
walkthrough.text: Player 2 now drafts a group with two gray tokens. They place one gray on top of an existing gray, creating a level two stack. On their next turn, they will try to get a third gray to reach level three for their mountain animal. This is how you build vertically toward height-dependent patterns.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Mistake: Covering a Pattern
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has two green tokens adjacent on the board, working toward the deer pattern. They draft a group with a gray token and decide to stack it on top of one of the green tokens. Now that space shows gray from above, not green. The deer pattern is broken! Player 1 will need another green token to fix this.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Think Before You Stack
walkthrough.text: Stacking changes the visible color of a space. Before placing a token on top of another, check whether the bottom color is part of a pattern you need. If it is, stacking there breaks that pattern. Only stack when the new color advances a different pattern that is worth more to you than the one you are covering.

walkthrough.title: Completing the First Animal
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has recovered and now has three adjacent green tokens at level one. That matches the deer card perfectly! Player 1 claims the deer card and scores those victory points. The green tokens stay on the board. Nice work completing your first animal pattern!

walkthrough.title: Drafting to Deny
walkthrough.text: Player 3 notices that Player 2 needs one more gray token to complete their mountain animal. The display has a group with a gray token that Player 2 would take next turn. Player 3 takes that group instead, denying Player 2 the gray. Competitive drafting is a valid and important strategy.

walkthrough.title: Late Game Decisions
walkthrough.text: As tokens run out, you need to decide whether to push for partially complete animals or abandon them. An incomplete animal scores zero points. If you are three tokens away from finishing a complex pattern with only two turns left, pivot to something achievable. Finished simple animals beat unfinished complex ones.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have learned how to draft tokens, build landscapes in three dimensions, and score animal patterns. Remember to place with purpose, think before you stack, and stay flexible about which animals to pursue. Harmonies rewards careful spatial planning.

walkthrough.title: Before You Explore
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to terrain types and scoring.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Choose Complementary Animals
walkthrough.text: When selecting animal cards, pick animals that share similar color requirements. If two animals both need green tokens, you can build overlapping patterns. Animals that need conflicting colors in the same spaces are impossible to complete simultaneously. Check your cards against each other before the game starts.

walkthrough.title: Build a Foundation First
walkthrough.text: In the early turns, focus on placing ground-level tokens that form the base of your landscape. You cannot stack to level two without a level one foundation. Identify where you need height and start building those foundation zones early so you are ready to stack when the right colors appear in the display.

walkthrough.title: Draft with Multiple Purposes
walkthrough.text: The best drafts serve multiple animal patterns at once. A group of two green and one yellow could advance both a forest animal and a meadow animal simultaneously. Look for groups where every token has a purpose. If a group has one useful token and two wastes, find a better option.

walkthrough.title: Height Planning
walkthrough.text: Animals with height requirements need early planning. A mountain eagle at level three needs three turns of gray stacking in the same spot. Start building those towers early so you are not scrambling in the late game. Ground-level animals are faster to complete, so balance your card selection.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Three mistakes trip up beginners. First, choosing animal cards that conflict with each other by needing different colors in the same area. Second, stacking tokens carelessly and breaking patterns you were building. Third, spreading tokens randomly instead of building concentrated zones that match your animal card layouts.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Abandon
walkthrough.text: If you are halfway through the game and an animal card looks impossible to complete, stop investing tokens in it. Incomplete animals score zero points. Redirect your efforts toward animals you can actually finish. A completed simple animal beats an incomplete complex one every time.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Display
walkthrough.text: The central display is shared information. Before your turn, scan what groups are available and plan which one you will take. Also notice what your opponents need. If you can take a group that both helps you and denies an opponent, that is a double win.

summary.title: Choose Complementary Animals
bullets: Pick animals with overlapping color needs
Avoid cards needing conflicting colors in same spaces

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't pick conflicting animal cards
Don't stack carelessly over needed colors
Don't scatter tokens randomly

summary.title: Know When to Abandon
bullets: Incomplete animals = 0 VP
Redirect efforts to finishable patterns

summary.title: Watch the Display
bullets: Plan your draft before your turn
Deny opponents when possible

walkthrough.title: Multi-Pattern Overlap
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring players build zones where tokens contribute to multiple animal patterns simultaneously. A cluster of green tokens at level one could satisfy a deer, a butterfly, and a forest bonus card all at once. Plan your board layout so that zones serve double or triple duty.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Stacking Order
walkthrough.text: When building height, the order of colors matters. Place the color you need at the visible top last. If you need gray at level three for a mountain eagle but green at level one for a forest deer, put the green down first. This way both patterns can be satisfied from the same column of tokens.

walkthrough.title: Landscape Bonus Optimization
walkthrough.text: Landscape bonus cards reward terrain coverage and height achievements. If a bonus rewards having four gray tokens at level three, plan your mountain building to hit that threshold. These bonuses can add significant points on top of your animal cards, especially if your terrain investments serve both purposes.

walkthrough.title: Denial Drafting
walkthrough.text: In competitive play, taking groups your opponents need is just as valuable as taking groups you need. Track which animals your opponents are building and identify which colors they need most. If a group would complete their pattern, consider hate-drafting it even if it is not your ideal pick.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Every token you place should score points. Calculate your token efficiency by dividing your total points by the number of tokens placed. High-scoring players average more points per token because they build overlapping patterns with shared tokens. Avoid placing tokens that only serve a single low-value pattern.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Token Pool
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, the token pool composition changes. If many gray tokens have been drafted already, future groups will contain fewer grays. This affects whether height strategies are viable. Track the rough color distribution in the bag and adjust your animal card priorities accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Pivots
walkthrough.text: In the final few turns, stop building for the future and start completing patterns now. Any partially built animal that will not finish is dead weight. Use your remaining drafts to grab the exact colors needed to close out your best scoring opportunities. Pivot ruthlessly and abandon lost causes.

entries.definition: Represents trees and forests. Commonly used at various heights for woodland animals.
Represents fields and meadows. Often used at ground level for prairie and grassland animals.
Represents mountains and rocky terrain. Frequently needed at level two or three for mountain-dwelling animals.
Represents settlements and structures. Used for animals associated with human habitations.
Represents water features. Usually placed at ground level for aquatic and waterside animals.
A token placed directly on an empty hex space on the board. The base layer of the landscape.
A token stacked on top of a level one token. Requires a foundation beneath it.
A token stacked on top of a level two token. The maximum height. Creates peaks and towers in the landscape.
A scoring card showing a specific pattern of colored tokens at specific heights. Claimed for VP when the pattern is completed on your board.
Optional scoring cards that reward specific terrain coverage or height achievements on your board.
Shared area where token groups are available for drafting. Refilled from the bag after each turn.
A set of three tokens in the central display. Players must take all three from one group on their turn.
Comparing the top-visible colors and heights on your board to the requirements on an animal card. All positions must match for completion.
Placing a token on top of another, changing the visible color of that space. Can complete or break existing patterns.
Personal player board with hexagonal spaces where tokens are placed. The foundation for building your 3D landscape.
